source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure That Instances Are Not Configured To Use the Default Service Account

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure That Instances Are Not Configured To Use the Default Service Account
input_row.description: It is recommended to configure your instance to not use the default Compute Engine service account because it has the Editor role on the project.
input_row.rationale: When a default Compute Engine service account is created, it is automatically granted the Editor role (roles/editor) on your project which allows read and write access to most Google Cloud Services. This role includes a very large number of permissions. To defend against privilege escalations if your VM is compromised and prevent an attacker from gaining access to all of your project, you should either revoke the Editor role from the default Compute Engine service account or create a new service account and assign only the permissions needed by your instance. To mitigate this at scale, we strongly recommend that you disable the automatic role grant by adding a constraint to your organization policy. The default Compute Engine service account is named [PROJECT_NUMBER]- compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the VM instances page by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/instances. 2. Click on each instance name to go to its VM instance details page. 3. Under the section API and identity management, ensure that the default Compute Engine service account is not used. This account is named [PROJECT_NUMBER]-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com. From Google Cloud CLI 1. List the instances in your project and get details on each instance: gcloud compute instances list --format=json | jq -r '. | "SA: \(.[].serviceAccounts[].email) Name: \(.[].name)"'  2. Ensure that the service account section has an email that does not match the pattern [PROJECT_NUMBER]-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com. Exception: VMs created by GKE should be excluded. These VMs have names that start with gke- and are labeled goog-gke-node.
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the VM instances page by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/instances. 2. Click on the instance name to go to its VM instance details page. 3. Click STOP and then click EDIT. 4. Under the section API and identity management, select a service account other than the default Compute Engine service account. You may first need to create a new service account. 5. Click Save and then click START. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Stop the instance: gcloud compute instances stop <INSTANCE_NAME> 2. Update the instance: gcloud compute instances set-service-account <INSTANCE_NAME> --service- account=<SERVICE_ACCOUNT> 3. Restart the instance: gcloud compute instances start <INSTANCE_NAME> Default Value: By default, Compute instances are configured to use the default Compute Engine service account. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/access/service-accounts 2. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/access/create-enable-service-accounts- for-instances 3. https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/compute/instances/set-service- account

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control can be fully verified via GCP APIs and Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) by checking the service accounts used by instances.
- The audit field provides commands using the Google Cloud SDK to list instances and verify which service account is being used.
- The process relies on evaluating standard GCP service properties (service accounts and the roles they have), which is checkable through GCP APIs.
- The Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) CLI provides the necessary commands to identify if a default service account is being used on instances, which makes it automatable.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_compute_instance_non_default_service_account_used

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command `gcloud compute instances list --format=json` to retrieve all instances and their associated service accounts.
- Process the returned JSON data to extract the service account emails associated with each instance.
- Verify that no instance is using a service account matching the pattern `[PROJECT_NUMBER]-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com`, except those excluded by the exceptions (e.g., instances with names starting with `gke-` and labeled `goog-gke-node`).
- Check that the service account is not the default Compute Engine service account.
- Pass/Fail Criteria: 
  - **Pass**: If an instance uses any service account other than the default Compute Engine service account.
  - **Fail**: If an instance uses the default Compute Engine service account `[PROJECT_NUMBER]-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com` and does not meet the exception criteria.
- The automation should iterate over all instances in a given project to ensure comprehensive evaluation of service account usage.

**SOURCE:** CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
step2_review:
---

### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is correct. This control can be fully checked via GCP APIs and Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) without requiring manual inspection.
- **GCP API/SDK/CLI Capability:**  
  The `gcloud compute instances list --format=json` command provides all necessary instance metadata, including the service account(s) attached to each VM. This data can be parsed to determine if the default Compute Engine service account is in use.
- **Other GCP Capabilities:**  
  While GCP Config (now called "Config Connector") and Asset Inventory API could also be leveraged, the gcloud CLI is sufficient and appropriate for this control.

---

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 8/10**

- **Automation Details:**  
  - The use of `gcloud compute instances list --format=json` is correct and provides the required data.
  - Parsing the JSON for `serviceAccounts` and matching against `[PROJECT_NUMBER]-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com` is technically accurate.
  - Exception handling for GKE node pools is mentioned, which aligns with CIS guidance.
- **Program Name:**  
  - `gcp_compute_instance_non_default_service_account_used` is descriptive but could be clearer.
  - It would be more precise to use:  
    `gcp_compute_instance_no_default_service_account`
- **Minor Issues:**  
  - The check should explicitly handle the case where an instance has **no service account** attached (which is also compliant).
  - The exception logic for GKE nodes could be more explicitly described (e.g., referencing both instance name prefix and labels).
  - The description could mention that the check should be performed **per project/region/zone** as appropriate.
- **Missing:**  
  - No mention of using the REST API (`compute.instances.list`) as an alternative for automation.
  - No explicit mention of handling paginated results if there are many instances.

---

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS

- **Explicitly Handle "No Service Account":**  
  The automation should consider instances with no service account as compliant (per CIS guidance).
- **Clarify GKE Exception Logic:**  
  The exception for GKE nodes should be more clearly defined:  
  - Instance name starts with `gke-` **AND** label `goog-gke-node` is present.
- **Alternative GCP API Methods:**  
  - The [Cloud Asset Inventory API](https://cloud.google.com/asset-inventory/docs/reference/rest/v1/assets/searchAllResources) could be used for large environments or cross-project checks.
  - The REST API (`compute.instances.list`) is also suitable for direct API integration.
- **Program Name Suggestion:**  
  - `gcp_compute_instance_no_default_service_account`
  - Or, for even more clarity:  
    `gcp_compute_instance_service_account_not_default`
- **Edge Cases:**  
  - Consider multi-service account attachment (rare, but possible in some scenarios).
  - Consider documenting how to handle service accounts with custom roles that mimic the default's permissions.

---

### 4. CONFIDENCE: **HIGH**

- The assessment is technically sound and aligns with GCP capabilities and CIS guidance.
- Minor improvements are suggested, but the core approach is correct.

---

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **Modify**

- **Keep the audit as Automated.**
- **Modify** the automation details to:
  - Explicitly state that instances with no service account attached are compliant.
  - Clarify the GKE node pool exception logic (both name prefix and label).
  - Suggest a more precise program name:  
    `gcp_compute_instance_no_default_service_account`
  - Optionally mention alternative API methods for large-scale or cross-project automation.

---

**Summary Table:**

| Section              | Assessment        | Notes                                                                                   |
|----------------------|------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Approach Validation  | AGREE            | Fully automatable via gcloud/API                                                        |
| Technical Accuracy   | 8/10             | Correct, but could clarify edge cases and exception logic                               |
| Improvements         | See above        | Explicitly handle "no service account", clarify GKE logic, suggest better program name  |
| Confidence           | HIGH             | Approach is sound, minor clarifications needed                                          |
| Final Recommendation | MODIFY           | Update automation details and program name                                              |

---

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identified that this control can be fully audited using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud). The service account assigned to each Compute Engine instance is directly exposed in the instance metadata, retrievable via `gcloud compute instances list --format=json` or the Compute Engine API. No manual inspection is required, and all necessary data is programmatically accessible. The only minor point of improvement is the program name, which should be more precise.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
gcp_compute_instance_no_default_service_account

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- GCP Resource/Service: Compute Engine VM Instances
- API/CLI Method: `gcloud compute instances list --format=json` or Compute Engine API `instances.list`
- Properties to Check:  
  - `serviceAccounts[].email` field for each instance
  - The default Compute Engine service account format: `[PROJECT_NUMBER]-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com`
- Pass Criteria:  
  - Each instance either:
    - Does NOT have a service account attached, OR
    - Uses a service account that is NOT the default Compute Engine service account (i.e., does not match `[PROJECT_NUMBER]-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com`)
    - (Optionally, per CIS, GKE node pools with specific labels may be excluded)
- Fail Criteria:  
  - Any instance is found using the default Compute Engine service account (`[PROJECT_NUMBER]-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com`), except for documented exceptions (e.g., GKE node pools if applicable).

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
There was no substantive disagreement; both AI opinions agreed on automation. The only adjustment was to use a more precise program name as suggested by the second opinion.

**SOURCE:** CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
